sourceMovie.title: Les Misérables
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Audiences latched onto Les Misérables (2012) as a history theatrical experience: big enough for a group watch, specific enough to reward a second viewing once you know where it's headed. The history and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: The Count of Monte Cristo
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Les Misérables clicked for you, The Count of Monte Cristo (2002) is a strong next stop: both trade in history and drama, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: The Duchess
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Les Misérables clicked for you, The Duchess (2008) is a strong next stop: both trade in history and drama, with emotional whiplash that feels honest. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Les Misérables?
faq.answer: Try The Count of Monte Cristo, The Duchess, Throne of Blood—each shares DNA with Les Misérables while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Les Misérables?
faq.answer: Les Misérables (2012) is best described as History, Drama. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Les Misérables worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.3/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this history lane.
